title: Sacramento, CA

raw_text:
```json
{
  "claims": [
    "California beekeepers lost 41.9% of their colonies last year, the second worst year on record.",
    "Pollinators are critical to many of California's leading crops, worth upwards of $11 billion annually.",
    "A huge body of research links adverse health impacts and the decline in pollinator populations to the use of pesticides, particularly neonicotinoids.",
    "Neonicotinoids are the world’s most widely used insecticides and are incredibly toxic to pollinators.",
    "Just one square foot of grass treated with a typical neonic lawn product can contain enough neonics to kill one million bees.",
    "Most neonicotinoid chemicals stay in the soil for years and are easily carried by rain or lawn watering.",
    "Key crops like almonds and strawberries depend on pollinators like bees, monarchs, and other birds and insects.",
    "Neonicotinoid pesticides cause harm to pollinators even at miniscule levels.",
    "Effective and less harmful alternatives to neonicotinoids are already available on the market.",
    "Studies show ubiquitous neonic exposure in pregnant women in California.",
    "Neonic exposure is linked to malformations of the developing heart and brain, reduced testosterone, and a variety of other reproductive and neurological effects.",
    "Most uses of neonicotinoids in non-agricultural settings are unnecessary."
  ]
}
```
